coredata.dc:title: N-modified carbon quantum dot in 3D-network of microfibrillated cellulose for building photoluminescent thin film as tartrazine sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Melvin Ng, H.K.
Lim, G.K.
Leo, C.P.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dot (CDs) dispersed in aqueous solution has been widely studied, this work developed photoluminescent thin film as tartrazine sensor by incorporating nitrogen modified CDs (NCDs) dispersed in three-dimensional network of microfibrillated cellulose (MFC) in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) thin film. CDs and NCDs was synthesized using microwave assisted hydrothermal method. MFC enhanced the dispersion of quantum dot and improved the luminescence intensity as shown in transmission electron microscopic images and ImageJ analysis results. The LOD of CDs was slightly impaired but the LOD of NCDs was slightly improved by MFC in tartrazine quenching. After drying and crosslinking, PVA/CD, PVA/CD-MFC, PVA/NCD, PVA/NCD-MFC thin films formed without defects and thickness less than 200 nm as shown in scanning electron microscopic images. However, the light transmittance of PVA/CD-MFC thin film was reduced comparison to PVA/CD thin film, resulting the smallest changes of fluorescence intensity during tartrazine quenching. PVA/NCD-MFC thin film showed the greatest reduction in photoluminescence which can be detected using image analysis. It offers data storage, transfer and analysis without liquid sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Microfibrillated cellulose
Polyvinyl alcohol
Tartrazine
Sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, NCDs and photoluminescent thin films
Characterization of CDs, NCDs and MFC composite
Characteristics of CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MFC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (a) CD, (b) CD-MFC, (c) NCD and (d) NCD-MFC.
From left to right: Images of CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MFC under (a) normal light and (b) UV light.
Total grey intensity of (a) CD, (b) CD-MFC, (c) NCD and (d) NCD-MFC images under normal light (left), UV light (middle) and green pixel intensity (right).

Linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity and the concentration of tartrazine of (a) CD, (b) CD-MFC, (c) NCD and (d) NC-MFC. Conditions: Concentrations of tartrazine were a-k: blank 10.00, 20.00, 30.00, 40.00, 50.00, 60.00, 70.00, 80.00, 90.00, 100.00 μM. Inset: the linear plot.
Light transmittance of PVA/CD, PVA/CD-MFC, PVA/NCD and PVA/NCD-MFC thin films.

Images of (a) empty vial, PVA/CD and PVA/CD-MFC thin films attached on empty vial (from left to right) as well as (b), (c) empty vial, PVA/NCD and PVA/NCD-MFC thin films attached on empty vial (from left to right) under normal light and UV light.
SEM images of the (a) PVA/CD, (b) PVA/CD-MFC, (c) PVA/NCD and (d) PVA/NCD-MFC thin films.
Fluorescence intensity of the (a) PVA/CD, (b) PVA/CD-MFC, (c) PVA/NCD and (d) PVA/NCD-MFC thin films before and after tartrazine quenching (10 mL of 10 μM for 30 min).
Grey colour intensity of PVA/NCD-MFC thin films before (left) and after (right) tartrazine quenching (10 mL of 10 μM for 30 min).
Composition of dope solution for the fabrication of photoluminescent thin film.

head.ce:title: N-modified carbon quantum dot in 3D-network of microfibrillated cellulose for building photoluminescent thin film as tartrazine sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14300 Nibong Tebal, Pulau Pinang, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14300 Nibong Tebal, Pulau Pinang, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14300 Nibong Tebal, Penang, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dot (CDs) dispersed in aqueous solution has been widely studied, this work developed photoluminescent thin film as tartrazine sensor by incorporating nitrogen modified CDs (NCDs) dispersed in three-dimensional network of microfibrillated cellulose (MFC) in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) thin film. CDs and NCDs was synthesized using microwave assisted hydrothermal method. MFC enhanced the dispersion of quantum dot and improved the luminescence intensity as shown in transmission electron microscopic images and ImageJ analysis results. The LOD of CDs was slightly impaired but the LOD of NCDs was slightly improved by MFC in tartrazine quenching. After drying and crosslinking, PVA/CD, PVA/CD-MFC, PVA/NCD, PVA/NCD-MFC thin films formed without defects and thickness less than 200 nm as shown in scanning electron microscopic images. However, the light transmittance of PVA/CD-MFC thin film was reduced comparison to PVA/CD thin film, resulting the smallest changes of fluorescence intensity during tartrazine quenching. PVA/NCD-MFC thin film showed the greatest reduction in photoluminescence which can be detected using image analysis. It offers data storage, transfer and analysis without liquid sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots (CDs) are photoluminescent carbon with particle size smaller than 10 nm in diameter. Their photoluminescent properties have attracted great interest in bioimaging [1,2], drug delivery [2], optronic and sensing [3]. This is because they are biocompatible, nontoxic, water soluble as well as flexible for modification. More importantly, they can be produced from a wide range of organic materials using bottom-up or top-down approaches. The bottom up approaches are hydrothermal synthesis, microwave irradiation, electrochemical carbonization and thermal decomposition, while the top-down approaches are electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, chemical ablation or oxidation, plasma treatment and ultrasonication. The applications of CDs as photoluminescence chemosensor, chemiluminescence chemosensor, electrochemiluminescence chemosensor, optical biosensor and photoelectrochemical biosensor have been extensively explored [3].

Instead of being dispersed in aqueous solution, CDs were coated on different substrates as reported by several researchers [4–7] in the past few years. CDs are coated on electrode as they enhanced electrochemical performance by increasing the electrical conductivity. Hence, the electrochemical analysis of adrenaline, serotonin and ascorbic acid can be simultaneously conducted [4]. The application of CDs coating as photoluminescence sensor was further reported. Boronic acid-functionalized CDs were immobilized on acrylate modified glass to form diacrylate polyethylene glycol/CDs composite under ultraviolet curing for the detection of dopamine [5]. The composite thin film showed high selectivity of dopamine despite the presence of other compounds. CDs have also been incorporated into flexible thin films for more applications. N-doped CDs were embedded into polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) thin film to form luminescent solar concentrators [6]. The optical properties of solar concentrators were affected by the layer number and CDs concentration. Polystyrene, poly(vinyl chloride) and polycarbonate dope solution containing CDs was spin coated on substrate to form highly luminescent thin films [8]. The photoluminescence properties were stable even after long-term UV exposure. Cueves et al. [7] impregnated regenerated cellulose thin film with silicon, carbon and nitrogen-doped CDs. The electrical resistance of the regenerated cellulose thin incorporated nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) was reduced more than 3 orders of magnitude, but the optical transmission of regenerated cellulose was not significantly affected. Our research team developed food colorant sensor by incorporating CDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) thin film [9]. The preliminary work revealed that PVA/CD thin film could detect tartrazine as low as 10 μM using a fluorescence spectrometer. However, the photoluminescence of PVA/CD thin film are difficult to be quantified using image analysis for developing smartphone-based sensing platform. Although NCDs with enhanced properties and higher quantum yield [10] could be the answer, Yuan at al [11]. Suggested to disperse CDs in three-dimensional graphene for improving photoluminescent intensity.

Similar to graphene, three-dimensional cellulosic network provides a great surface for CDs dispersion. Cellulose, a biopolymer abundantly available has been used to prepare photoluminescent thin film as well. Cotton cellulose blended with CDs and ZnO was electrospun to form photosensitive thin film [12]. The photosensitive thin film showed light transmittance about 75%. Cellulose can be further transformed into useful nano building blocks for photoluminescent thin films, namely cellulose nanofibers (CNF) or microfibrillated cellulose (MFC), cellulose nanocrystals (CNC), and bacterial nanocellulose (BNC). Among the cellulose derivatives, MFC with three-dimensional network that possesses high viscosity and yield stress are useful for producing durable thin film. Moreover, the three-dimensional network helps to disperse nanoparticles in thin film [13,14]. Quraishi et al. [15] anchored amino-modified CDs on nanocellulose gels via aqueous carbodimimide coupling. Unlike the nanocellulose gel with physically bonded CDs, it can be converted into aerogel by solvent exchange and supercritical CO2 drying without affecting light transmittance significantly. Wu et al. [16] grafted amino-modified CDs on CNF aerogels with carboxyl groups via condensation reaction initiated by N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and N-hydroxysuccinimide. The freeze-dried composite containing 3% of CDs showed strong fluorescence quenching by nitric oxide gases, formaldehyde, glutaraldehyde under UV fluorescent image. Tabatabaee et al. [17] quantified the photoluminescence of CDs coated on bacterial nanocellulose paper after bilirubin isomerization under the blue light for hyperbilirubinemia diagnosis using smartphone. Cheng et al. [18] immersed microcrystalline cellulose aerogel into polyethyleneimine modified CDs solution to develop iron sensor.

In this work, CDs and NCDs were dispersed in the three-dimensional network of MFC before transformed into photoluminescent thin film which can work as a sensor. The interaction between CDs or NCDs with MFC was first compared in this work. After composite characterization, tartrazine quenching using these composites were studied. The composites were later incorporated into hydrophilic PVA thin film to form photoluminescent thin film. Herein, the photoluminescent thin film can work as a sensor with (i) photoluminescence spectra for quantitative analysis and (ii) photoluminescence image for simple and quick qualitative analysis.

Citric acid (≥ 99%, Merck), urea (≥ 99.5%, Merck) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4, anhydrous, R&M chemicals) were used to synthesis CDs. Microfibrillated cellulose (MFC, Exilva F01-L) sponsored by Borregaard Cellulose Fibrils was used in the preparation of photoluminescent composite. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA with average molecular weight of 31,000–50000, 87–89% hydrolyzed) acquired from Sigma was used in the preparation of durable composite film. Ethanol (≥ 99.4%) was supplied by Merck while tartrazine was acquired from R & M Chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, NCDs and photoluminescent thin films
ce:section.ce:para: Microwave assisted method was used to synthesize CDs. Citric acid (carbon source) and K2HPO4 were dissolved together in distilled water at a mass ratio of 5:1:10 as reported elsewhere [19]. The precursor solution as much as 10 mL was stirred using a magnetic stirrer (WiseStir MSH–20D) at 300 rpm for 30 min. The solution was then heated in microwave (Samsung MW71e) at 750 W for 150 s. The resulting brownish solid was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water with stirring for another 1 h at 300 rpm. Centrifugation was carried out at 4000 rpm for 30 min (Eppendorf 5702 R) to remove suspended solid and the supernatant were further purified using 0.45 μm syringe filter to remove large particles or agglomerate [20]. NCDs precursor was prepared by dissolving 1.051 g of citric acid and 0.3 g of urea into 10 mL of distilled water [21]. The solution was then processed as mentioned above to obtain NCDs.

Polymeric thin film was prepared as followed. CDs solutions were heated to 90 °C and PVA was added slowly under stirring at 500 rpm. Citric acid and MFC (dissolved in distilled water) were added into the mixture as a cross-linker and additive, respectively. The mixture was continuously stirred for 4 h until PVA and citric acid were completely dissolved and dispersed. On the other hand, NCDs solution was first diluted with distilled water until it reached the similar photoluminescent intensity with CDs solution. The composition of dope solutions for various thin films were prepared according to the list in Table 1 .

The dope solution was degassed using a planetary mixer (Kurabo KK-300SS) with 1 min mixing at 1000 rpm followed by 1 min degassing at 1000 rpm to eliminate air bubble and sedimentation. The photoluminescent thin films were prepared by pouring 5 g of dope solution on the Petri dish. These films were dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight to remove the water. The thin films were peeled off and cured at 140 °C for 4 h. Circular samples with a diameter of 2 cm were used in the quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, NCDs and MFC composite
ce:section.ce:para: The CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MCF samples were diluted with ethanol and dropped on a copper grid coated with holey carbon film. The microstructure of CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MFC samples was observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, 200 kV with Field Emission, TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN, FEI). Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis (Oxford Instruments, England) and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS10 with scanning in the range of 4000–650 cm−1) of these samples were used to study the elemental composition and functional groups, respectively. The fluorescence properties of these samples were determined using a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer LS55) with the excitation wavelength set at 350 nm. The scanning was conducted at a scan speed of 1000 nm per min as well as at the excitation and emission widths of 15 nm each. The interaction between CDs and MFC as well as NCDs and MFC were observed by adding the varied concentration of MFC in CDs or NCDs solution. The quenching was carried out by adding 0.5 mL of known concentrations of tartrazine into 10 mL of diluted sample.

FTIR spectra of photoluminescent thin films were collected using a FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS10) in the range of 4000–650 cm−1. The transparency of the film in term of light transmittance within a range of 200–1000 nm through the thin films was measured using was a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Carry 60, Agilent). The thin films were cracked after being immersed in liquid N2 for the morphology and structure study using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 200 FESEM, FEI). The photoluminescence of these thin films were measured using the fluorescence spectrometer as mentioned previously, with the aid of solid sample holder accessory with 1% light transmittance setting. The sensing properties of these photoluminescent thin films were tested using 10 mL of tartrazine solution with a concentration of 10 μM. After being immersed in the tartrazine solution for 30 min, the photoluminescent thin films were dried and the quenching was studied using the fluorescence spectrometer and ImageJ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MFC
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and NCDs with spherical shape were successfully synthesized as shown HRTEM image presented in Fig. 1 (a) and (c). CDs and NCDs with particle size smaller than 10 nm agglomerated and formed clusters that are larger than 100 nm. The agglomeration of CDs was also reported by Yuan at al. [11] who suggested the improvement of CDs dispersion in three-dimensional graphene. With the presence of MFC, CDs and NCDs adhered on the chains/branches of MFC network in CD-MFC and NCD-MFC samples as shown in Fig. 1 (b) and (d). MFC not only offered a great surface area for the coherence, but also provided aid in the dispersion of CDs and NCDs. Feng et al. [22] reported on the similar finding during the assembly of CDs, oxidized nanocellulose and ZnO nanostructures. Tang et al. [23] controlled the carboxylate content of nanocellulose and the molar ratio of Cd2+ to carboxylate content to further improve the dispersion of CdS quantum dot in nanocellulose. EDX analysis in Fig. S1 shows that CD sample mainly consists of C and O elements, with small quantity of P and K elements from the residue of K2HPO4. NCD sample contains 10.81 wt% of N element, implying the nitrogen doping. FTIR spectra in Fig. S2 shows that CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MFC samples possess OH functional group represented by the broad spectrum within 3000–3600 cm−1 and CO group represented by the sharp peak around 1630 cm−1 [21,24]. The NH peak could npt be determined since it overlapped with the broad peak of −OH.

To further compare the functionality of CD, CD-MFC, NCD, NCD-MFC samples, quenching was conducted using tartrazine (Fig. 4 ). NCD sample was diluted 20 times using distilled water before quenching so that the similar photoluminescent intensity reading was achieved in the beginning of comparison. All samples showed a linear relationship in tartrazine quenching due to Förster resonance energy transfer as reported by other researchers [25]. It was noted that CD and CD-MFC samples have broader peaks compared to NCD and NCD-MFC samples. Furthermore, the peaks of CD and CD-MFC samples decreased slower than the peaks of NCD and NCD-MFC samples after reaching the highest peak at around 430 nm. A slight blue shift was observed for NCD sample that was modified with urea. NCD sample exhibited a shorter wavelength emission when it was excited at 350 nm compared to CD sample as reported by other [26,27]. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using:
(1) LOD = 3σ/K
Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 5) and K is the slope of the calibration graph (Inset graph in Fig. 3). Based on the calculation results, CD, CD-MFC, NCD and NCD-MFC samples synthesized in this work achieved LOD of 3.32, 4.22, 3.38, and 2.61 ppm, respectively. The effects of MFC on the LOD of CD and NCD are different, MFC could increase or decrease LOD depending on the doping of CD. Such effects require more detailed studies to design tartrazine sensor that matches the regulatory limits of tartrazine in different food products, namely 30–300 ppm under Malaysia Food Regulations 1985 [28].

SEM was used to study the cross section of photoluminescent thin films prepared in this work. SEM images of these photoluminescent thin films presented in Fig. 7 shows dense and non-porous morphology. All the photoluminescent thin films possess thickness less than 200 nm. CD, NCDs and MFC are considerably compatible to PVA since they did not create any defects in the thin films. The finding is consistent with the recent studies where the addition of CDs has no significant effect on thin film formation mechanism either involving drying or coagulation [34,35].

The chemical properties of the photoluminescent films were studied using FTIR spectra. As shown in FTIR spectra (Fig. S8), all the photoluminescent thin films exhibit the similar patterns because the addition of CDs, NCDs and/or MFC did not affect much on the functional group of PVA. The broad peak occurred in the range of 3700–3000 cm−1 was due to the symmetrical stretching vibration of −OH functional groups which are abundant in PVA [27,35]. A small peak around 3000-2900 cm−1 was caused by the stretching of C–H groups. The CO stretching vibration of ester bond were also detected at 1700 cm−1, while the carboxyl CO groups and symmetric CC stretching were observed around 1150–1300 cm−1, respectively [36]. It is difficult to distinguish the peaks caused MFC with −OH, −CH and −CH2 groups, CDs with −OH, −COOH and −CH2 groups or NCDs with −OH, −COOH and –NH2 groups added at a small quantity [27].

A simple microwave assisted hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs and NCDs from citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The addition of MFC in the CDs and NCDs solutions enhanced the dispersion of quantum dot and improve the luminescence intensity. The presence of MFC affected the LOD of CDs in tartrazine sensing negatively but improved the LOD of NCDs. PVA/CD, PVA/CD-MFC, PVA/NCD, PVA/NCD-MFC thin films without defects and thickness less than 200 nm were successfully synthesized. The photoluminescent properties of CDs and NCDs with or without MFC was retained after blending into the PVA thin film. In comparison to PVA/CD thin film, the light transmittance of PVA/CD-MFC thin film was slightly affected by the incorporation of MFC. Hence, PVA/CD-MFC thin film showed the smallest changes of fluorescence intensity during tartrazine quenching. PVA/NCD-MFC thin film showed the greatest reduction in photoluminescence after tartrazine adsorption, and it could be detected using image analysis. This thin film is potential candidate for developing smartphone-based sensing platform. It can be also used to store the chemical information and transfer the data without liquid sample for quantitative analysis.
None.

H.K. Melvin Ng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft. G.K. Lim: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision. C.P. Leo: Conceptualization, Resources, Data Curation, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge Malaysia education ministry for supporting this research via Program Translational MRUN-Rakan RU (MRUN- Rakan RU-2019-004/2, 203, PJKIMIA/6720018). Finally, we would like to thank Borregaard Cellulose Fibrils for providing MFC. Universiti Sains Malaysia is acknowledged for providing Graduate Assistant Scheme to Mr. Melvin Ng Hau Kwan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.112286.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots for on-off-on detection of Hg2+ and glutathione in aqueous medium: live cell imaging and implication logic gate operation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.K. Singh, V. Singh, P.K. Yadav, S. Chandra, D. Bano, B. Koch, M. Talat, S.H. Hasan, Nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots for on-off-on detection of Hg2+ and glutathione in aqueous medium: Live cell imaging and IMPLICATION logic gate operation, J. Photochem. Photobiol., A, 384 (2019) 112042.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesized multiple fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as an efficient label-free optical nanoprobe for in vivo live-cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, S. Perumal, N.C.S. Selvam, Y.R. Lee, Green synthesized multiple fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as an efficient label-free optical nanoprobe for in vivo live-cell imaging, J. Photochem. Photobiol. A, 372 (2019) 99-107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Li, T. Chen, J.J. Gooding, J. Liu, A Review of Carbon and Graphene Quantum Dots for Sensing, ACS Sens., (2019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot-modified carbon paste electrode-based sensor for selective and sensitive determination of adrenaline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Shankar, R.M. Shereema, V. Ramachandran, T. Sruthi, V.S. Kumar, R. Rakhi, Carbon quantum dot-modified carbon paste electrode-based sensor for selective and sensitive determination of adrenaline, ACS Omega, 4 (2019) 7903-7910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free detection of dopamine based on photoluminescence of boronic acid-functionalized carbon dots in solid-state polyethylene glycol thin film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.-Y. Lee, S. Munir, S.-Y. Park, Label-free detection of dopamine based on photoluminescence of boronic acid-functionalized carbon dots in solid-state polyethylene glycol thin film, Macromol. Res., 26 (2018) 1150-1159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots based nanocomposite thin film for highly efficient luminescent solar concentrators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, X. Zhao, Z. Guo, P. Miao, X. Gong, Carbon dots based nanocomposite thin film for highly efficient luminescent solar concentrators, Org. Electron., 62 (2018) 284-289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eco-friendly modification of a regenerated cellulose based film by silicon, carbon and N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cuevas, B. Campos, R. Romero, M. Algarra, M.I. Vázquez, J. Benavente, Eco-friendly modification of a regenerated cellulose based film by silicon, carbon and N-doped carbon quantum dots, Carbohydr. Polym., 206 (2019) 238-244.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of highly luminescent composites by polymer embedding of carbon dots derived from N-hydroxyphthalimide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.S. Stan, P.G. Horlescu, L.E. Ursu, M. Popa, C. Albu, Facile preparation of highly luminescent composites by polymer embedding of carbon dots derived from N-hydroxyphthalimide, J. Mater. Sci., 52 (2017) 185-196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-dot dispersal in PVA thin film for food colorant sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.N.H. Kwan, C. Leo, S.A. Senanayake, G. Lim, M. Tan, Carbon-dot dispersal in PVA thin film for food colorant sensing, J. Environ. Chem. Eng., (2019) 103187.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Indian gooseberry-derived tunable fluorescent carbon dots as a promise for in Vitro/in vivo multicolor bioimaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N. Jebakumar Immanuel Edison, S. Perumal, Y.R. Lee, Indian Gooseberry-Derived Tunable Fluorescent Carbon Dots as a Promise for In Vitro/In Vivo Multicolor Bioimaging and Fluorescent Ink, ACS Omega, 3 (2018) 17590-17601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small nitrogen-doped carbon dots as efficient nanoenhancer for boosting the electrochemical performance of three-dimensional graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Yuan, X. Zhao, Y. Liang, L. Peng, H. Dong, Y. Xiao, C. Hu, H. Hu, Y. Liu, M. Zheng, Small nitrogen-doped carbon dots as efficient nanoenhancer for boosting the electrochemical performance of three-dimensional graphene, J. Colloid Interface Sci., 536 (2019) 628-637.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Pan, X. Zhang, C. Zhao, S. Xie, Y. Zheng, C. Cui, C. Li, The flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO, Mater. Lett. 211 (2018) 289-292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Translucent and adsorptive PVA thin film containing microfibrillated cellulose intercalated with TiO2 nanoparticles for dye removal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.M. Ng, C. Leo, Translucent and adsorptive PVA thin film containing microfibrillated cellulose intercalated with TiO2 nanoparticles for dye removal, Colloids Surf., A, (2019) 123590.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The coherence between TiO2 nanoparticles and microfibrillated cellulose in thin film for enhanced dispersal and photodegradation of dye

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.M. Ng, C. Leo, The coherence between TiO2 nanoparticles and microfibrillated cellulose in thin film for enhanced dispersal and photodegradation of dye, Prog. Org. Coat., 132 (2019) 70-75.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical versus physical grafting of photoluminescent amino-functional carbon dots onto transparent nematic nanocellulose gels and aerogels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Quraishi, S.F. Plappert, T. Grießer, W. Gindl-Altmutter, F.W. Liebner, Chemical versus physical grafting of photoluminescent amino-functional carbon dots onto transparent nematic nanocellulose gels and aerogels, Cellulose, (2019) 1-16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent aerogels based on chemical crosslinking between nanocellulose and carbon dots for optical sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wu, G. Zhu, A. Dufresne, N. Lin, Fluorescent aerogels based on chemical crosslinking between nanocellulose and carbon dots for optical sensor, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 11 (2019) 16048-16058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Easy diagnosis of jaundice: a smartphone-based nanosensor bioplatform using photoluminescent bacterial nanopaper for point-of-care diagnosis of hyperbilirubinemia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.S. Tabatabaee, H. Golmohammadi, S.H. Ahmadi, Easy diagnosis of jaundice: a smartphone-based nanosensor bioplatform using photoluminescent bacterial nanopaper for point-of-care diagnosis of hyperbilirubinemia, ACS Sens., 4 (2019) 1063-1071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots/hydrogel nanocomposite with stable Fe3+ sensing capability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Cheng, M. Xing, Q. Wu, Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots/hydrogel nanocomposite with stable Fe3+ sensing capability, J. Alloys Compd., 790 (2019) 221-227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers on carbon quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of tetracycline in milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hou, H. Li, L. Wang, P. Zhang, T. Zhou, H. Ding, L. Ding, Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers on carbon quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of tetracycline in milk, Talanta, 146 (2016) 34-40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Betel-derived nitrogen-doped multicolor carbon dots for environmental and biological applications
